critic_raw:
This CNN vs. MLR crop yield prediction design has a critical split validity problem and several structural flaws.

The described 'spatio-temporal stratified split' is incoherent as specified. The design says 60/20/20 splits are achieved by 'randomly sampling field-seasons while maintaining proportions of high and low yield fields.' If field-seasons are randomly assigned to train/val/test, then the same field can appear in all three sets (in different seasons), and the same season can appear in all three sets (across different fields). This means the test set is not truly held-out: spatial autocorrelation between fields means that training on a neighboring field in the same season provides direct spatial leakage; temporal autocorrelation within a field means that training on prior seasons provides yield trend information. The design claims to respect 'temporal and spatial structure' but the described random sampling procedure does neither. A genuine spatial-temporal split would assign entire fields or entire seasons to non-overlapping partitions.

PCA is fit on the training set only — which is correctly stated — but the CNN also receives hyperspectral image patches. The CNN is processing raw spatial image patches, not the PCA-reduced features. The prompt contradicts itself: it says PCA components are derived from the training set and applied to validation/test, but it also describes the CNN as processing '2D convolutional neural network operating on hyperspectral image patches.' If the CNN takes image patches as input, PCA preprocessing is irrelevant to the CNN branch. If the CNN takes PCA-reduced features as input, this is a non-standard CNN design that requires clarification. As written, both models may not be operating on the same feature representation.

The spatial leakage risk from field ID embeddings is specifically acknowledged but not resolved. Including field ID as an embedding in training while evaluating on some field-seasons from those same fields allows the model to memorize field-level yield baselines rather than learning hyperspectral-to-yield mappings. The design notes this but only proposes it as a mitigation strategy, not a verified control.

The 'persistence baseline' predicts each field's average yield from the validation season — but this is a temporally privileged baseline that uses Season 2 (validation) data to predict Season 3 (test). If the intent is a naive lower bound, it should predict from Season 1 data, not validation data. Using validation data for the naive baseline is inconsistent with the holdout protocol.

RMSE is selected as the primary metric with bootstrapped confidence intervals, but there is no correction for the spatial autocorrelation in residuals. Moran's I is proposed as a secondary diagnostic, but if residuals are spatially autocorrelated, the effective sample size for the bootstrap is smaller than the number of field-season observations, and the reported confidence intervals will be too narrow.
all_issues_raised: The 'spatio-temporal stratified split' randomly assigns field-seasons to splits, allowing the same field and same season to appear in train and test — this does not prevent spatial or temporal leakage and contradicts the stated goal of respecting data structure.

The CNN processes raw hyperspectral image patches while MLR receives PCA-reduced features; the two models operate on different input representations, so any performance difference conflates representation quality with model architecture.

Field ID embeddings are included as features without excluding test fields from training, enabling the model to memorize field-level yield means rather than learning generalizable spectral-yield relationships.

The persistence baseline uses Season 2 (validation) data to compute field means for predicting Season 3 test performance, giving it access to data beyond the training window and inflating its apparent accuracy.

Bootstrap confidence intervals for RMSE do not account for spatial autocorrelation in residuals; if Moran's I is significant, the effective sample size is reduced and reported intervals are anticonservatively narrow.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 'spatio-temporal stratified split' randomly assigns field-seasons to splits, allowing the same field and same season to appear in train and test — this does not prevent spatial or temporal leakage and contradicts the stated goal of respecting data structure.

The CNN processes raw hyperspectral image patches while MLR receives PCA-reduced features; the two models operate on different input representations, so any performance difference conflates representation quality with model architecture.

Field ID embeddings are included as features without excluding test fields from training, enabling the model to memorize field-level yield means rather than learning generalizable spectral-yield relationships.

The persistence baseline uses Season 2 (validation) data to compute field means for predicting Season 3 test performance, giving it access to data beyond the training window and inflating its apparent accuracy.

Bootstrap confidence intervals for RMSE do not account for spatial autocorrelation in residuals; if Moran's I is significant, the effective sample size is reduced and reported intervals are anticonservatively narrow.
